concept_name: Velocity-Time Graphs — Slope Gives Acceleration, Area Gives Displacement
source_book: DC Pandey Chapter 7.5 — v-t graphs; split from motion_graphs bundle

real_world_anchor.primary: A Mumbai-Pune Expressway toll booth. A lorry starts at rest (v = 0), then presses the accelerator — the v-t graph is a rising straight line, slope = acceleration. Cruise at 80 km/h for five minutes — a flat horizontal line at 80. Then braking into the next booth — v-t line drops with a steep negative slope. The AREA under this v-t curve from booth to booth is exactly the distance travelled, roughly 50 km. Every driver intuitively reads a v-t graph through their speedometer; we just draw the same story on paper.
real_world_anchor.secondary: A Bangalore Metro train departing Cubbon Park. For the first 15 seconds the velocity rises linearly from 0 to 60 km/h — that linear rise is the acceleration. The NEXT 90 seconds the velocity is perfectly flat at 60 km/h — zero acceleration, pure cruising. Last 15 seconds it drops from 60 to 0 — deceleration. The triangular + rectangular + triangular area under this trapezoidal v-t plot IS the station-to-station distance.
real_world_anchor.tertiary: A cricketer hitting a six. Immediately after contact the ball has maximum upward velocity. As it rises, gravity pulls it down — v decreases linearly with slope = -g. At the peak v = 0, and on the way down v becomes negative (moving down), and it keeps growing in magnitude. The v-t plot is a single straight line crossing the t-axis at the peak. The area above t-axis = distance up; area below = distance down; both equal if air resistance is ignored.

formulas.slope_is_accel: slope of v-t graph at any point = dv/dt = acceleration at that instant
formulas.area_is_displacement: Area between v-t curve and t-axis from t1 to t2 = integral v dt = displacement
formulas.area_sign: Area above t-axis is positive displacement; area below t-axis is negative displacement
formulas.triangle_area: For constant acceleration from rest, v = at, so distance = (1/2) t * v = (1/2) a t^2

constraints: Horizontal line on v-t = constant velocity = zero acceleration
Straight sloping line on v-t = uniform (constant) acceleration
Parabolic curve on v-t = non-uniform acceleration
Velocity going below t-axis means object has reversed direction
Rectangular area = v * t = distance for uniform motion

STATE_1.title: v-t Graph — Reading the Slope as Acceleration

scene_composition.text:
Velocity-Time graph (v vs t)

v |              ______
  |            /
  |          /
  |        /
  |      /        slope = a
  |    /
  |  /
  |/____________________  t
  0

  Slope of v-t  =  dv/dt  =  acceleration

    Flat line on v-t -> constant velocity -> a = 0
    Uphill line on v-t -> speeding up -> a > 0
    Downhill line on v-t -> slowing down -> a < 0
Mumbai-Pune Expressway: lorry accelerating from a toll booth

tts_sentences.text_en: A velocity-time graph plots how fast you are moving against the clock.
The single most important rule on a v-t graph: the slope of the line, at any point, equals the acceleration at that instant.
Picture a lorry leaving a toll booth on the Mumbai-Pune Expressway. It starts at zero and steadily picks up speed.
On the v-t graph, that steady pick-up is a straight line rising from the origin. The steeper the line, the harder the lorry is accelerating.
Once the lorry cruises at 80 km/h, the velocity is constant, so the v-t line becomes perfectly flat. Zero slope means zero acceleration, not zero speed.

STATE_2.title: v-t Graph — Area Under the Curve is Displacement

scene_composition.text:
Area under v-t = Displacement

v |
  |           _______________________
  |          |                       |
  |          |       AREA = distance |
  |          |                       |
  |          |___v = 60 km/h_________|
  |         /|                       |\
  |        / |                       | \
  |       /  |                       |  \
  |      /   |                       |   \
  |_____/____|_______________________|____\___  t
  0   start  t1                      t2   stop
     accel   cruise                      decel

  For uniform motion (rectangle): distance = v * t
  For accel from rest (triangle): distance = (1/2) * t * v_final
  Total displacement = sum of these regions.
Bengaluru Metro Cubbon Park -> MG Road: 120 s trip

tts_sentences.text_en: The second power of a v-t graph: the area under the curve, between any two moments, equals the displacement in that interval.
Take a Bengaluru Metro train leaving Cubbon Park station. It accelerates for 15 seconds, cruises at 60 km/h for 90 seconds, and decelerates for 15 seconds into M G Road.
On the v-t graph, that trip is a flat-topped trapezium — a triangle going up, a rectangle on top, and a triangle coming down.
Sum the three areas — half times 15 times 60, plus 90 times 60, plus half times 15 times 60 — and you get the full station-to-station distance, in the same time units.
Shape tells you acceleration from slope. Region tells you distance from area. Two powers, one diagram.

STATE_3.title: When Velocity Crosses the Axis — Reversing Direction

scene_composition.text:
v-t graph crossing the t-axis

v |
  |    up_phase               
  |   /|                       
  |  / |          top of throw
  | /  |         /
  |/___|________/________________  t
  |     \      /
  |      \    /
  |       \  /
  |        \/ down_phase (velocity negative)
  |

  Area above the t-axis = upward distance
  Area below the t-axis = downward distance
  Their sum (with sign) = net displacement

  Where the line crosses the t-axis is the moment the ball
  momentarily stops at the top of its throw.
Cricket six: ball thrown straight up, then falls

tts_sentences.text_en: Here is the edge case students often miss. The v-t line can cross the time axis.
Think of a cricketer hitting the ball straight up. The moment after contact, velocity is strongly positive — upward.
Gravity slows the ball at a constant rate, so the v-t line is a perfectly straight line with slope minus g.
At the top of the flight the line crosses zero — the ball is stationary for a split second, then the velocity turns negative as the ball falls back.
Triangle above the axis is the distance up. Triangle below is the distance down. If they are equal, the ball lands where it started — net displacement zero.

epic_c_branches.misconception_summary: Student thinks the area under a v-t graph gives velocity, not displacement.
confusion_patterns: area under v-t graph is velocity
what does area under vt graph mean

STATE_1.title: The Trap: Area Looks Like It Might Be Velocity

scene_composition.text:
Student's (wrong) idea:

  "The curve shows velocity,
   so the area under it must also be velocity!"

v |____________
  |           |
  |  area = ? |
  |           |
  |___________|_____  t

  Student points at the area and says:
    'That's just a bigger patch of velocity.'

  Dimension check coming next...

tts_sentences.text_en: Before we correct this, let us state the belief plainly.
The student sees velocity plotted on the vertical axis, so anything measured on that graph feels like velocity.
If the line itself is velocity, the area underneath must be… more velocity? That is the trap.
We will break the trap with two moves: a units check, and a concrete Metro journey.

STATE_2.title: Units Force the Answer: m/s Multiplied by s Equals m

scene_composition.text:
Check the units of the area:

   height of strip = velocity  (m/s)
   width  of strip = time      (s)
   strip area      = (m/s) * s = m

   So the area MUST have units of metres,
   which is displacement — not velocity.

   Every horizontal slice you add under the curve
   contributes another little bit of distance,
   summed across the whole interval.
The s in the denominator of m/s cancels with the s of time.

tts_sentences.text_en: Units are the fastest way to settle this.
Every thin vertical strip under the v-t curve has height velocity, in metres per second.
Its width is a tiny duration, in seconds. Their product is metres — displacement, not velocity.
So the area under a v-t curve is ALWAYS in metres, no matter what shape the curve is. It is distance, not speed.

STATE_3.title: Concrete Check: Metro Train Area = Distance in Metres

scene_composition.text:
Bengaluru Metro: 60 km/h cruise for 90 s

   area of cruising rectangle
      = 60 km/h * 90 s
      = (60 * 1000 / 3600) m/s * 90 s
      =  16.67 m/s * 90 s
      =  1500 m  =  1.5 km

   That is the distance covered while cruising.
   It is NOT 1500 km/h or 1500 (km/h)^2.
   It is simply 1.5 kilometres.

tts_sentences.text_en: Let us burn the correct reading into memory with one concrete number.
Take the Metro cruising at 60 km per hour for 90 seconds — that is 16 point six seven metres per second times 90 seconds.
That product is 1500 metres — one and a half kilometres of track covered. A distance, plain and simple.
So whenever you look at a v-t graph, say aloud: slope is acceleration, area is displacement. Never swap those two.
